chunk_summary: This section discusses evaluating growth velocity and pubertal tempo in school-age children and adolescents, emphasizing the importance of growth patterns, pubertal staging, and clinical interpretation to identify potential growth issues or delays.
learning_objectives: Understand the typical growth velocity in mid-childhood and its clinical significance.
Learn how pubertal staging impacts growth interpretation and timing.
Identify clinical signs that warrant referral for growth failure evaluation.
Recognize the role of diet, sleep, and chronic illness management in supporting growth.
key_facts: Typical height velocity in mid-childhood (3 years to puberty) is 5–6 cm/year.
Persistent growth velocity below 5 cm/year with downward centile crossing suggests pathology.
Pubertal staging is critical: early puberty may shorten growth potential, while delayed puberty can mimic growth failure.
Proportionate growth with stable tracking and delayed but normal puberty may not require intervention.
Improving diet, sleep, anemia, and chronic illness control can enhance growth velocity but does not guarantee adult height.
reference_quote: In mid-childhood (about 3 years to puberty), typical height velocity is roughly 5–6 cm/year; persistent velocity clearly below this, especially with downward centile crossing, suggests pathology or sustained constraint that warrants evaluation.
document_key_topics: Growth velocity in mid-childhood and adolescence
Impact of pubertal timing on growth patterns
